summary: DHH reflects on Jason Fried's observations from the Web 2.0 conference, noting that the excitement and VC-heavy atmosphere felt like a replay of the 1999 dot-com bubble. He argues that the real promise of Web 2.0 is that cheap computing power and bandwidth mean companies don't need massive funding or headcount to build great products. The post advocates for staying small and self-funded rather than chasing venture capital rounds.
key_points: The Web 2.0 conference atmosphere of VC money and funding rounds echoed the 1999 dot-com bubble
Cheap bandwidth, computing power, and better tools mean companies can postpone or avoid organizational scaling
Self-funding and small teams are the real advantage of the Web 2.0 era, not raising millions
money_quotes: What's so freaking special about Web 2.0 is that you don't need to let the organizational scaling get ahead of the business.
All this newfound computing power, cheap bandwidth, and powerful technology are enablers for postponing the growth of heads.
Keep it small. Start small and stay small. Borrow from yourself before you borrow from someone else.
You can have an impact with just a few people. You can build great products with a small team. You can do it on your own. You can.
key_insight: The true promise of Web 2.0 isn't another VC-fueled boom but the ability for small, self-funded teams to build great products without scaling headcount.
